deeperExplanation: Tuples are slightly faster and consume less memory than lists because CPython allocates exact memory size once during creation. A tuple is hashable (has a `__hash__()` method) ONLY if all of its contained elements are also hashable. If a tuple contains a mutable list `(1, [2, 3])`, the tuple itself cannot be hashed!
internalImplementation: CPython maintains a free-list cache for small tuples (up to size 20) to speed up creation/allocation.

examples.code:
# Tuple with Mutable Objects
tup = (1, [2, 3])
tup[1].append(4)  # Modifies nested list inside tuple!
print(tup)         # (1, [2, 3, 4])
# hash(tup)        # TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
examples.explanation: While tuple reference array is immutable, nested mutable objects inside can still mutate, making tuple unhashable.

performanceConsiderations.spaceComplexity: Saves 16-24 bytes memory compared to equivalent list.

debuggingWalkthrough.bugDescription: Creating single element parenthesis expression instead of tuple.
debuggingWalkthrough.correctCode:
item = ('apple',) # Trailing comma creates tuple
print(type(item)) # <class 'tuple'>

bestPractices: Use tuples for heterogeneous records `(name, age, email)`.
Use namedtuples (`collections.namedtuple`) for self-documenting tuple fields.
